title: The Dungeon Dive's Top 50 Games of All Time* - part 5
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5kFO9eUjRPk

The summit. Before the countdown, Daniel (@dungeondive) lays out a business plan — moving to three days a week at his part-time job, aiming for one thousand patrons at five dollars a month, plus podcast ads, merch, dungeon-synth albums, and solo RPG modules on DriveThruRPG. Eventually a small DiveCon. Then: the top ten.

**10. DungeonQuest** — the press-your-luck reduction of every dungeon crawl to its core elements. **9. Talisman 2nd Edition** — nostalgia and Gary Chalk art; played correctly it's a *race* game. **8. Shadows of Brimstone** — the Warhammer Quest derivative that actually nails it. **7. Escape the Dark Castle** — Daniel's pick for closest-to-perfect on the list, treated as a party game. **6. Xplore-It: Valley of the Dead King** (the simplest entry — slow questing perfected). **5. Machina Arcana** — overflowing with theme, well worth the learning curve. **4. Sleeping Gods** — could move to #1; the perfect balance of book and play. **3. Warhammer Quest: Silver Tower** — narrow focus is the feature, not the bug. **2. Warhammer Quest (1995)** — the toolbox the entire genre is still chasing. **1. Secrets of the Lost Tomb** — garish, gaudy, audacious, ugly; Hong Kong action cinema in box form, and the most fun he's ever had at a table.

----

Daniel ranks Secrets of the Lost Tomb above Warhammer Quest because flaws and verve beat polish for him. Where do *you* sit on the polish-vs-personality axis when ranking your own favourites?
